author: @jerryshay221
text: People fear what they do not understand.

author: @judyofthewoods
text:
There are a number of sensible questions one can ask. At what dose do I sustain irreversible damage? In lower doses, is it cumulative in the body or can the body rid itself from it? Is it dangerous in combination with some other substance? Is it biodegradable in the environment in a reasonably short period of time, or is it a persistent toxin? Is there an antidote or method of degrading it into harmless substances? How can I minimise exposure/use/need/damage? What is the alternative? Not for people who's world is black and white.
As an aside, there was an item on the news only a few days ago about  woman who died from drinking too much water. She was in psychiatric care and staff didn't notice that she was drinking excessive amounts of water until it was too late - coma from hyponatremia, followed by death.

author: @mfr58
text: One of the problems we have is that our houses are full of materials off gassing a cocktail of different, potentially toxic, chemicals 24/7 and in our centrally heated, air tight, energy efficient homes, life is not going top be so healthy....I've experienced health problems from this myself....

author: @TomFarrell-p9z
text: Had several relatives pumped full of the stuff and they aren't complaining!  Except for Great Uncle Louie, who died of lead poisoning, during prohibition.  Got on the wrong end of a Chicago typewriter, but that's a different story.  🙂

author: @industrialmonk
text: I remember in the early 1970s processing  photo films & darkroom process as a small child with my dad & having special chemical handling & neutralising chemical's & then later self neutralising chemical's & the safety becoming simpler  & it still fascinates me but thay all have to be treated with appropriate care.

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: You spoiled my joke in the description of the video , because I wanted to ask you , what kind of moisturizing cream are you using , because you look at least 6 years younger in this video. Never mind... On the point of dangerous chemicals , what would people do , if lemonade is labeled as such and such solution of citric acid , or whiskey is fuel/mild solvent with tannin and stuff . Never mind the home cleaning products . It is a matter of social degradation and civilization decline. Of course , nobody should mess with things he , or she doesn't understand , at least on a basic level. Seems to me , that common sense is long since out of fashion , for many people.

replies.author: @partciudgam8478
replies.text: you are taking fun out of the hands of 90% of the people! how much persons do you think basically understands how does something as commonplace as a microwave oven?, let alone a fuel injection car or a computer!

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: @partciudgam8478 I believe that government(s) are responsible for taking out fun from people's lives. For an example , where I live , we are allowed to make our own spirits (mostly fruit brandy and wine - unless it is meant for sale , in which case there is a lot of regulations and laws that have to be applied). However , that means , that we know how to produce alcohol drinks without methyl , because we don't want to poison ourselves. Further more , people in developing and undeveloped countries (majority of world's population), all over the world , know how to fix things , or make things they need (stick and string principle) , because they can't buy new (or they can't afford them). So , it just depends on particular places on the planet (mostly big cities of developed countries) , that you're referring to. It only takes two complacent generations , living very comfortable life , to mess up a society. But , human kind (besides some insects) is the most adaptable kind on the planet (we survived the ice age)  , so no worries.

replies.text: @gordonauld5945I mean, it's certainly dangerous for you if you try to breathe it :) I've done that more times than I'd like to admit. Plus , I'm weird. I actually like the taste of Dasani.

author: @Ken00001010
text: I can see that removing lead from buidings exposes more people (mostly workers) to lead during the time of removal than leaving it alone during that time. However,  I am not sure that represents more exposure to the general population than the long time deterioration over decades does. If not removed in a controlled way, all the lead will eventually go into the environment uncontrolled and expose future generations. Perhaps future generations can build robots to remove the lead we leave in place now (i.e. with no human worker exposure), but I don't think many will be able to afford those.

author: @hughstan1
text:
Sounds like the bubble wrap safety brigade is having a crack at you as of late.  Well, just so you know 99% of us appreciate all you do even without the safety pandering.   I’ve supported your educational and Funning around content for along time and will continue to do so for the foreseeable future. Cheers from the States. 
Take care

author: @adamcrofts58
text: I was given a chainsaw, and my sister said instantly be careful. I pointed out to her that I had worked around and with fast moving machines all  my working life and the last thing I was going to do was not to treat it other than a very lethal tool. Complacency around machines leads but to injury.

author: @hempshadz
text: Robert, any chance I can pick your brain or come to some sort of agreement, so that I can obtain some of the results from your Hemp / Graphene/ Tea experiments. I'm having a hard time getting my plastic manufacture to produce a composite that can be used for sunglass frames. The current ratio I'm told by my injection mold Co. is to brittle.  They suggest I use TR-90. I was hopping I could get the Hemp composite to have similar attributes to the TR-90. Any thoughts??

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: @hempshadz I'd suggest asking via email. You can't be sure if/when he might read your comment.

replies.author: @hempshadz
replies.text: @VeniceInventors Thank you for the suggestion, maybe my email  hit the spam folder. I'll try with a personal E-mail, I don't want to be a bug a boo. lol Unfortunately I can't seem to convey what I need to the plastic co my wife has been dealing with. I'm hoping Robert could set me on the right path or recommend someone. Thanks tho.

author: @bertoldriesenteil1430
text: When people "use chainsaws and they shouldn't" it isn't  always "their problem". When the chainsaw belongs to me and the person who borrowed it didn't top up the oil, and now it is a heap of spare parts, it is my problem !
